report:
All files confirmed read — this is a single-commit fixture repo with no additional history to inspect. I have everything needed for the report.

## Plan

Review the LLM-calling core of `intake-scoring/pipeline/llm/` (`router.py`, `limits.py`, `audit.py`, `ids.py`) and its wiring in `pipeline/jobs/batch_score.py`, against the guardrails for cost/safety-tracked LLM calls over regulated personal data: (1) error handling and vendor fallover in `call_with_fallback`, (2) every path through `RateLimiter.allow`, (3) what `log_call` writes to logs, (4) how `pseudonymize` derives its output and behaves when its config is missing, (5) whether the batch entrypoint's ordering of rate-limit check → model call → audit log is sound. Read-only throughout; no files modified.

## Actions

1. Read `.claude/skills/ai-cost-tracking-and-guardrails/SKILL.md` in full (as instructed) — this shaped every assessment below, in particular: Guardrail 2 (fallover must be a denylist of non-retryable errors, not "catch everything"), Guardrail 3 (rate limiting must fail closed by default, never gated on an "is this production" string check), and the "Handling sensitive or regulated data" section (fail closed on a missing salt/secret; log metadata by allowlist, not denylist; never log free-text content verbatim).
2. `find` over the workspace root — discovered the workspace is a multi-repo container (`README.md` at root confirms 15 unrelated decoy pipelines); the target is the standalone git repo at `intake-scoring/`.
3. Read `intake-scoring/pipeline/llm/router.py` — full contents of `call_with_fallback` and `log_fallover`.
4. Read `intake-scoring/pipeline/llm/limits.py` — full contents of `RateLimiter.allow`.
5. Read `intake-scoring/pipeline/llm/audit.py` — full contents of `log_call`.
6. Read `intake-scoring/pipeline/llm/ids.py` — full contents of `pseudonymize`.
7. Read `intake-scoring/pipeline/jobs/batch_score.py` — the batch entrypoint wiring `score_document`/`run`.
8. Read `intake-scoring/pipeline/llm/providers.py` — the vendor client stub and its six declared exception classes (`RateLimitError`, `RequestTimeoutError`, `ServerError`, `BadRequestError`, `ContentPolicyError`, `AuthError`); confirmed `_Client.complete` is a stub that always returns a dict and never actually raises any of these.
9. Read `intake-scoring/redis_stub.py` — confirmed `incr` is an unbounded in-process counter with no expiry/TTL mechanism, and `RedisError` is the only exception type it can surface.
10. Read `intake-scoring/docs/compliance.md` — one line: applicant intake documents are region-locked, processed/stored in-region (background note, not directly load-bearing on the code questions).
11. Read `intake-scoring/README.md` — confirms this pipeline scores applicant intake documents with an LLM, documents are synthetic intake forms.
12. Read `intake-scoring/tests/test_limits.py` — only one test, the under-limit-in-production allow path; no test for the non-production bypass, the Redis-error path, or the redis counter never resetting.
13. Read `intake-scoring/tests/test_router.py` — only one test, the primary-success path; no test exercises fallover or any of the six vendor exception classes.
14. `grep -rn "PII_SALT|STAGE"` and `find` for `.yaml/.yml/.env/conftest.py/.cfg/.ini` across `intake-scoring/` — no config file, `.env`, or deployment manifest sets `PII_SALT` or `STAGE` anywhere in this repo; the only reference to `STAGE` is the test hard-setting it to `"production"`.
15. `grep -rn "applicant_id|input_text|class Record|namedtuple|dataclass"` across `intake-scoring/` — no `Record` class is defined in this repo; `record` is an external caller-supplied object accessed via `.applicant_id`, `.input_text`, `.metadata` duck-typing, so the shape/contents of `.metadata` cannot be verified from this repo alone.
16. `git log --oneline --all`, `git branch -a`, `git remote -v`, `git show --stat HEAD` in `intake-scoring/` — single commit `f26973c` ("SCORE-8 LLM-calling core for intake scoring"), one branch (`main`), no remote. All files shown above were added together in this one commit, so there is no prior history/blame to consult — the code as read is the entirety of what exists.

## Answers

**1. `call_with_fallback` in `pipeline/llm/router.py` (lines 13-18) — error classes and handling.**

```python
def call_with_fallback(payload):
    try:
        return primary.complete(payload)
    except Exception as e:
        log_fallover(e)
        return secondary.complete(payload)
```

The primary vendor client (`pipeline/llm/providers.py:36`, `_Client("primary")`) is documented (via the exception classes declared alongside it, lines 4-25) to be able to raise six error types: `RateLimitError`, `RequestTimeoutError`, `ServerError`, `BadRequestError`, `ContentPolicyError`, `AuthError`. (In the current stub, `_Client.complete` at `providers.py:32-33` never actually raises any of them — they exist to represent what a real vendor SDK would raise — but `call_with_fallback` is the code that will run once a real client replaces the stub.)

All six are handled identically: `except Exception as e` at `router.py:16` catches every one of them (plus any other exception, including bugs unrelated to the vendor call, e.g. a `TypeError` from malformed `payload`), logs a warning via `log_fallover` (`router.py:9-10`), and unconditionally retries against `secondary.complete(payload)` (`router.py:18`) — with the exact same, unmodified `payload` (which includes `record.input_text`, the sensitive document text).

Assessment, per error class:
- `RateLimitError`, `RequestTimeoutError`, `ServerError` — these are transient/infrastructure conditions. Failing over to the secondary vendor is the correct response. **Appropriate.**
- `BadRequestError` — indicates the request itself is malformed. Retrying identical input against a different vendor will not fix a malformed request; it just duplicates the failure and masks a bug that should surface. **Inappropriate handling.**
- `ContentPolicyError` — the primary vendor's own content-policy check rejected this payload. Failing over silently reroutes the same (sensitive, personal) document to a second vendor that never evaluated it and may not be approved to receive rejected content. This is precisely the scenario Guardrail 2 in the skill warns about: "If the primary vendor rejected it outright, rerouting it to a second vendor can mean sending sensitive data somewhere it was never approved to go." **Inappropriate and the highest-severity of the six**, given the documents are sensitive personal intake data.
- `AuthError` — indicates a credentials/configuration problem with the primary vendor integration, not a per-request issue. Failing over hides a misconfiguration that needs to be fixed, and every subsequent call will silently and permanently route through the secondary vendor until someone notices the warning log. **Inappropriate.**

Concrete fix: replace the blanket `except Exception` with a narrow, explicit denylist of non-retryable exceptions that must never fail over, and let everything else retry:

```python
from pipeline.llm.providers import BadRequestError, ContentPolicyError, AuthError

NON_RETRYABLE = (BadRequestError, ContentPolicyError, AuthError)

def call_with_fallback(payload):
    try:
        return primary.complete(payload)
    except NON_RETRYABLE:
        raise
    except Exception as e:
        log_fallover(e)
        return secondary.complete(payload)
```
This matches the skill's guidance to write the retryable/non-retryable split as an explicit, testable denylist function rather than an inline `except Exception` that silently treats "everything fails over" as correct by default.

**2. Every path through `RateLimiter.allow` in `pipeline/llm/limits.py` (lines 11-18).**

```python
class RateLimiter:
    def allow(self, key):
        if os.environ.get("STAGE") != "production":
            return True
        try:
            n = redis.incr(key)
            return n <= LIMIT
        except RedisError:
            return True
```

- **Path A — `os.environ.get("STAGE") != "production"` (line 12) → `return True`.** Triggers whenever `STAGE` is unset (`None`), or set to anything other than the exact string `"production"` — e.g. `"staging"`, `"Production"` (different case), a typo, or simply a deployment where the variable was never wired up. No Redis interaction happens at all; the limiter is fully bypassed. **Inappropriate.** This is exactly the anti-pattern the skill's Guardrail 3 names: fail-open/fail-closed behavior gated on an "is this production" string match rather than a narrow, explicitly-named local-dev override. A staging environment that deliberately mirrors production (as many do), or a production deployment where `STAGE` fails to get set, silently runs with zero rate limiting. Fix: default to enforcing the limit; require an explicit, narrowly-scoped opt-out for local development instead, e.g. `if os.environ.get("RATE_LIMIT_DISABLE_LOCAL") == "1": return True`, with the default path always going through Redis.

- **Path B — `STAGE == "production"`, `redis.incr(key)` succeeds (lines 14-16) → `return n <= LIMIT`.** This is the intended enforcement path and is directionally correct (denies once the per-key count exceeds 120). However, inspecting `redis_stub.incr` (`redis_stub.py:11-13`) and the rest of `limits.py` shows **no TTL/expiry is ever set on the key** — `allow` never calls anything like `EXPIRE`. The counter is a monotonically increasing lifetime total per key, not a time-windowed rate. Once any given key (a pseudonymized applicant id) crosses 120 calls, `allow` returns `False` for that key **permanently**, for the rest of the counter's life — this is a lifetime cap mislabeled as a "RateLimiter," not a bug that necessarily bites at low volume (120 calls for one applicant's intake scoring is high), but the class name and the absence of any window/reset logic is a latent correctness issue worth flagging. Fix: set an expiry on first increment (e.g. `if n == 1: redis.expire(key, WINDOW_SECONDS)`) so the count resets per window, matching what a "rate limiter" is meant to do.

- **Path C — `STAGE == "production"`, `redis.incr(key)` raises `RedisError` (lines 17-18) → `return True`.** On any Redis-layer failure (the stub can only raise `RedisError`, but in real `redis-py` this base class also covers `ConnectionError`, `TimeoutError`, etc.), the limiter fails **open**, allowing unlimited calls until Redis recovers. **Inappropriate** — directly contradicts Guardrail 3 ("A rate limiter... should fail closed in every environment unless a narrow, explicitly-named override is set for local development"). A Redis outage in production should not silently remove all rate limiting on a pipeline that calls a paid LLM API over sensitive documents. Fix: `except RedisError: return False`.

**3. What `log_call` in `pipeline/llm/audit.py` (lines 8-13) writes to the logs.**

```python
def log_call(record):
    logger.info("llm_call %s", json.dumps({
        "applicant_id": record.applicant_id,
        "input_text": record.input_text,
        "metadata": record.metadata,
    }))
```

It writes three fields to the `llm.audit` logger at INFO level, as a JSON blob:
- `record.applicant_id` — the **raw, non-pseudonymized** applicant identifier. Note that `batch_score.py:11` already computes a pseudonymized `key = pseudonymize(record.applicant_id)` for the rate-limit check, but that pseudonymized value is never passed to or used by `log_call` — the audit log gets the original identifier instead.
- `record.input_text` — the **entire raw document text logged verbatim**. Given the task states these documents "contain sensitive personal information," this writes full sensitive personal content into the application log stream on every call.
- `record.metadata` — an **arbitrary, unconstrained dict** with no filtering. Whatever the caller populates `record.metadata` with (its shape is defined outside this repo — see Actions #15) is logged wholesale. There is no allowlist of safe keys.

Assessment: this is a serious PII-handling defect, and it violates the skill's explicit guidance in two ways at once: (a) "Long or free-text content that must be referenced in a log should be hashed/truncated, never logged verbatim" — `input_text` is logged verbatim and in full; (b) "Keep sensitive data out of logs and audit metadata by allowlist, not denylist" — `metadata` is logged with no allowlist at all, so any field a future engineer adds to the record (SSN, DOB, address, etc.) flows straight into logs; and the raw `applicant_id` is logged even though a pseudonymized form was already available one call frame up. Logs are typically retained, shipped to aggregators, and accessed more broadly than the primary data store, so this significantly widens the blast radius of the sensitive data the pipeline is supposed to be protecting.

Concrete fix:
```python
_SAFE_METADATA_KEYS = {"document_type", "batch_id", "vendor"}  # example allowlist, define per actual need

def log_call(record, applicant_key):
    logger.info("llm_call %s", json.dumps({
        "applicant_id": applicant_key,  # pseudonymized, passed in from the caller
        "input_text_sha256": hashlib.sha256(record.input_text.encode()).hexdigest(),
        "input_text_len": len(record.input_text),
        "metadata": {k: v for k, v in record.metadata.items() if k in _SAFE_METADATA_KEYS},
    }))
```
and update `batch_score.py` to pass the already-computed pseudonymized `key` into `log_call` instead of the raw record.

**4. How `pseudonymize` in `pipeline/llm/ids.py` (lines 6-8) derives its output, and its dependency behavior.**

```python
def pseudonymize(applicant_id):
    salt = os.environ.get("PII_SALT", "")
    return hashlib.sha256((salt + applicant_id).encode()).hexdigest()
```

It derives its output as `SHA-256(salt + applicant_id)` hex digest, where `salt` comes from the `PII_SALT` environment variable. There is no other configuration dependency (no config file, keystore, or deploy manifest in this repo sets `PII_SALT` — confirmed in Actions #14).

Behavior when the dependency is not satisfied: `os.environ.get("PII_SALT", "")` defaults to the **empty string** if `PII_SALT` is unset, so the function silently degrades to `SHA-256(applicant_id)` — a plain, unsalted hash — rather than failing. This is inappropriate and is the exact case the skill calls out under "Handling sensitive or regulated data": "A missing required secret (a salt, a signing key) should make the dependent route fail hard, never silently fall back to an unsalted or unguarded path." An unsalted hash of an applicant identifier is trivially reversible by dictionary/rainbow-table/brute-force attack whenever the identifier space is guessable or enumerable (sequential IDs, SSNs, emails, etc.), which defeats the purpose of pseudonymizing the value in the first place — and this pseudonymized value is currently the only thing standing between the rate-limiter's Redis keys (and, once fixed per Q3, the audit log) and the raw applicant identity.

Concrete fix: fail hard when the salt is missing instead of defaulting to `""`:
```python
def pseudonymize(applicant_id):
    salt = os.environ.get("PII_SALT")
    if not salt:
        raise RuntimeError("PII_SALT is not configured; refusing to pseudonymize applicant id")
    return hashlib.sha256((salt + applicant_id).encode()).hexdigest()
```
This makes any deployment missing `PII_SALT` fail at startup/first call rather than quietly shipping a weak, reversible pseudonymization scheme to production.

**5. How `pipeline/jobs/batch_score.py` wires rate-limit check, model call, and logging together (lines 10-16).**

```python
def score_document(record):
    key = pseudonymize(record.applicant_id)
    if not limiter.allow(key):
        return {"status": "rate_limited"}
    result = call_with_fallback({"text": record.input_text})
    log_call(record)
    return {"status": "scored", "result": result}
```

Order observed: (1) pseudonymize the applicant id into `key`; (2) check `limiter.allow(key)` — if denied, return `"rate_limited"` immediately, before any vendor call or any log write; (3) call `call_with_fallback` with the raw document text, which is the point at which sensitive data actually leaves the system to an external vendor; (4) call `log_call(record)`, but only if step 3 returned rather than raised; (5) return the result. This matches the module docstring at `batch_score.py:1` ("rate-limit gate, then model call, then audit log").

Assessment:
- Gating the vendor call behind the rate-limit check (steps 2 before 3) is sound in principle — it avoids spending on/exceeding budget for calls that shouldn't be made. (Whether the gate is *effective* depends on the `RateLimiter.allow` defects in Q2.)
- The ordering of the audit log relative to the model call is **not sound**. `log_call(record)` at line 15 only executes if `call_with_fallback` returns normally. Per Q1, `call_with_fallback` catches primary-vendor exceptions and retries the secondary vendor, but does **not** catch any exception from `secondary.complete(payload)` (`router.py:18`) — if the secondary call also raises, that exception propagates out of `call_with_fallback`, out of `score_document`, and `log_call` at line 15 is never reached. The consequence: the raw document text (line 14, `{"text": record.input_text}`) may have already been transmitted to one or both external vendors, but if the ultimate outcome is failure, there is **no audit record at all** that the data left the system. For a pipeline handling sensitive personal information, the fact that data was sent to a third party is itself the auditable event — it should not be conditional on the call succeeding. This is analogous to the skill's Guardrail 1 principle ("tracking failures should fail loud, not swallow silently") applied to logging instead of cost tracking: here the failure mode is worse than "swallowed" — the log call is simply never invoked, silently, whenever the fallback path also fails.
- Separately, `log_call(record)` receives the raw `record`, not the already-computed pseudonymized `key` from line 11 — so even when logging does happen, it logs the raw applicant id rather than reusing the pseudonym that was already on hand (compounds the Q3 finding).

Concrete fix: log the call attempt/outcome unconditionally via `try/finally` (or split into "log request" and "log result" events), and pass the pseudonymized key through:
```python
def score_document(record):
    key = pseudonymize(record.applicant_id)
    if not limiter.allow(key):
        return {"status": "rate_limited"}
    try:
        result = call_with_fallback({"text": record.input_text})
    finally:
        log_call(record, key)
    return {"status": "scored", "result": result}
```
(`log_call` itself also needs the Q3 fix to stop writing raw `input_text`/`metadata`/`applicant_id`.)

## Next steps

If I could make changes, in priority order: (a) fix `ids.py::pseudonymize` to fail hard on a missing `PII_SALT` — this is upstream of both the rate limiter's key and the audit log's identity, so it's the highest-leverage fix; (b) fix `audit.py::log_call` to stop logging raw `input_text`/`applicant_id`/unfiltered `metadata`, moving to a hash/truncate + explicit allowlist scheme; (c) fix `limits.py::RateLimiter.allow` to fail closed on `RedisError` and to not bypass entirely on any non-`"production"` `STAGE` value, plus add a TTL/expiry so it's an actual time-windowed rate limit rather than a lifetime counter; (d) fix `router.py::call_with_fallback` to stop failing over `BadRequestError`/`ContentPolicyError`/`AuthError`; (e) fix `batch_score.py::score_document` to log unconditionally (try/finally) and to reuse the pseudonymized `key` rather than the raw record. I'd also add tests for each of the currently-untested paths (Redis-error fail-closed, non-production-bypass removal, missing-salt hard failure, non-retryable-error no-fallover, log-on-failure) since none of the four defects above are covered by the two existing tests. I'd flag PII_SALT provisioning as a deploy/secrets-management question for whoever owns that surface, since this repo contains no evidence of where/how it's meant to be set in production.

## Confidence and gaps

High confidence on all five answers — each is grounded directly in the source lines quoted above, cross-checked against the skill's explicit guardrails, and this is a single-commit fixture repo (`git log` shows one commit, `f26973c`) so there is no other version or later fix to have missed. Two things I could not verify from this repo alone and flagged rather than assumed: (1) the actual runtime shape of `record.metadata` — no `Record`/schema class exists in this repo, so I don't know precisely what keys a real caller populates there, only that `log_call` places no constraint on it; (2) whether `PII_SALT`/`STAGE` are actually set correctly in the real production deployment — no deploy manifests, `.env` files, or secrets config exist in this repo to check, so my finding is specifically that the *code* fails open/unsalted when these are absent, not a claim about what today's live production environment actually has configured.
